title: Catalysis

infobox.value: ([/kəˈtælɪsɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [*kə-TAL-iss-iss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is the increase in [rate](/wiki/Reaction_rate "Reaction rate") of a [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") due to an added substance known as a **catalyst**[[1]](#cite_note-IUPACc-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([/ˈkætəlɪst/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KAT-əl-ist*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")). Catalysts are not consumed by the reaction and remain unchanged after the reaction.[[3]](#cite_note-3) If the reaction is rapid and the catalyst is recycled quickly, a very small amount of catalyst often suffices;[[4]](#cite_note-4) mixing, surface area, and temperature are important factors in reaction rate. Catalysts generally react with one or more [reactants](/wiki/Reactant "Reactant") to form [intermediates](/wiki/Reaction_intermediate "Reaction intermediate") that subsequently give the final reaction product, in the process of regenerating the catalyst.

of a catalyst is the [katal](/wiki/Katal "Katal"), which is quantified in moles per second. The productivity of a catalyst can be described by the [turnover number](/wiki/Turnover_number "Turnover number") (TON) and the catalytic activity by the *turn over frequency* (TOF), which is the TON per time unit. The biochemical equivalent is the [enzyme unit](/wiki/Enzyme_unit "Enzyme unit"). For more information on the efficiency of enzymatic catalysis, see the article on *[enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme#Kinetics "Enzyme")*.

change the extent of a reaction: they have **no** effect on the [chemical equilibrium](/wiki/Chemical_equilibrium "Chemical equilibrium") of a reaction. The ratio of the forward and the reverse reaction rates is unaffected (see also [thermodynamics](/wiki/Thermodynamics "Thermodynamics")). The [second law of thermodynamics](/wiki/Second_law_of_thermodynamics "Second law of thermodynamics") describes why a catalyst does not change the chemical equilibrium of a reaction. Suppose there was such a catalyst that shifted an equilibrium. Introducing the catalyst to the system would result in a reaction to move to the new equilibrium, producing energy. Production of energy is a necessary result since reactions are spontaneous only if [Gibbs free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy") is produced, and if there is no energy barrier, there is no need for a catalyst. Then, removing the catalyst would also result in a reaction, producing energy; i.e. the addition and its reverse process, removal, would both produce energy. Thus, a catalyst that could change the equilibrium would be a [perpetual motion machine](/wiki/Perpetual_motion_machine "Perpetual motion machine"), a contradiction to the laws of thermodynamics.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Thus, catalysts **do not** alter the equilibrium constant. (A catalyst can however change the equilibrium concentrations by reacting in a subsequent step. It is then consumed as the reaction proceeds, and thus it is also a reactant. Illustrative is the base-catalyzed [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") of [esters](/wiki/Ester "Ester"), where the produced [carboxylic acid](/wiki/Carboxylic_acid "Carboxylic acid") immediately reacts with the base catalyst and thus the reaction equilibrium is shifted towards hydrolysis.)
change the energy difference between starting materials and products (thermodynamic barrier), or the available energy (this is provided by the environment as heat or light).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Process of increasing the rate of a chemical reaction

"Catalyst" redirects here. For other uses, see [Catalyst (disambiguation)](/wiki/Catalyst_(disambiguation) "Catalyst (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Catalist](/wiki/Catalist "Catalist") or [*Cathalistis*](/wiki/Cathalistis "Cathalistis"). For the stage of metabolism, see [Catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Catalysts.JPG/250px-Catalysts.JPG)](/wiki/File:Catalysts.JPG)

A range of industrial catalysts in pellet form

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Low_Temperature_Oxidation_Catalyst.jpeg/250px-Low_Temperature_Oxidation_Catalyst.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Low_Temperature_Oxidation_Catalyst.jpeg)

An [air filter](/wiki/Air_filter "Air filter") that uses a low-temperature oxidation catalyst to convert [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") to less toxic [carbon dioxide](/wiki/Carbon_dioxide "Carbon dioxide") at room temperature. It can also remove [formaldehyde](/wiki/Formaldehyde "Formaldehyde") from the air.

**Catalysis** ([/kəˈtælɪsɪs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"), [*kə-TAL-iss-iss*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")) is the increase in [rate](/wiki/Reaction_rate "Reaction rate") of a [chemical reaction](/wiki/Chemical_reaction "Chemical reaction") due to an added substance known as a **catalyst**[[1]](#cite_note-IUPACc-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) ([/ˈkætəlɪst/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*KAT-əl-ist*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")). Catalysts are not consumed by the reaction and remain unchanged after the reaction.[[3]](#cite_note-3) If the reaction is rapid and the catalyst is recycled quickly, a very small amount of catalyst often suffices;[[4]](#cite_note-4) mixing, surface area, and temperature are important factors in reaction rate. Catalysts generally react with one or more [reactants](/wiki/Reactant "Reactant") to form [intermediates](/wiki/Reaction_intermediate "Reaction intermediate") that subsequently give the final reaction product, in the process of regenerating the catalyst.

The rate increase occurs because the catalyst allows the reaction to occur by an alternative mechanism which may be much faster than the noncatalyzed mechanism. However the noncatalyzed mechanism does remain possible, so that the total rate (catalyzed plus noncatalyzed) can only increase in the presence of the catalyst and never decrease.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Catalysis may be classified as either [homogeneous](/wiki/Homogeneous_catalysis "Homogeneous catalysis"), whose components are dispersed in the same phase (usually gaseous or liquid) as the reactant, or [heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogeneous_catalysis "Heterogeneous catalysis"), whose components are not in the same phase. [Enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") and other biocatalysts are often considered as a third category.

Catalysis is nearly ubiquitous in [chemical industry](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Estimates are that 90% of all commercially produced chemical products involve catalysts at some stage in the process of their manufacture.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The term "catalyst" is derived from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") [καταλύειν](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BA%CE%B1%CF%84%CE%B1%CE%BB%CF%8D%CF%89 "wikt:καταλύω"), *kataluein*, meaning "loosen" or "untie". The concept of catalysis was invented by chemist [Elizabeth Fulhame](/wiki/Elizabeth_Fulhame "Elizabeth Fulhame"), based on her novel work in oxidation-reduction experiments.[[8]](#cite_note-Laidler-8)[[9]](#cite_note-American_Chemical_Society-9)

## General principles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: General principles")]

### Example

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Example")]

An illustrative example is the effect of catalysts to speed the decomposition of [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide") into water and [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"):

:   2 H2O2 → 2 H2O + O2

This reaction proceeds because the reaction products are more stable than the starting compound, but this decomposition is so slow that hydrogen peroxide solutions are commercially available. In the presence of a catalyst such as [manganese dioxide](/wiki/Manganese_dioxide "Manganese dioxide"), this reaction proceeds much more rapidly. This effect is readily seen by the [effervescence](/wiki/Effervescence_(chemistry) "Effervescence (chemistry)") of oxygen.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The catalyst is not consumed in the reaction, and may be recovered unchanged and re-used indefinitely. Accordingly, manganese dioxide is said to *catalyze* this reaction. In living organisms, this reaction is catalyzed by [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") (proteins that serve as catalysts) such as [catalase](/wiki/Catalase "Catalase").

Another example is the effect of catalysts on air pollution and reducing the amount of carbon monoxide. Development of active and selective catalysts for the conversion of carbon monoxide into desirable products is one of the most important roles of catalysts. Using catalysts for the hydrogenation of carbon monoxide helps remove this toxic gas and produce useful materials.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Units

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Units")]

The [SI derived unit](/wiki/SI_derived_unit "SI derived unit") for measuring the **catalytic activity** of a catalyst is the [katal](/wiki/Katal "Katal"), which is quantified in moles per second. The productivity of a catalyst can be described by the [turnover number](/wiki/Turnover_number "Turnover number") (TON) and the catalytic activity by the *turn over frequency* (TOF), which is the TON per time unit. The biochemical equivalent is the [enzyme unit](/wiki/Enzyme_unit "Enzyme unit"). For more information on the efficiency of enzymatic catalysis, see the article on *[enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme#Kinetics "Enzyme")*.

### Catalytic reaction mechanisms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Catalytic reaction mechanisms")]

Main article: [Catalytic cycle](/wiki/Catalytic_cycle "Catalytic cycle")

In general, chemical reactions occur faster in the presence of a catalyst because the catalyst provides an alternative [reaction mechanism](/wiki/Reaction_mechanism "Reaction mechanism") (reaction pathway) having a lower [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy") than the noncatalyzed mechanism. In catalyzed mechanisms, the catalyst is regenerated.[[12]](#cite_note-LM82-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Steinfeld-15)

As a simple example occurring in the gas phase, the reaction 2 SO2 + O2 → 2 SO3 can be catalyzed by adding [nitric oxide](/wiki/Nitric_oxide "Nitric oxide"). The reaction occurs in two steps:

:   2 NO + O2 → 2 NO2 (rate-determining)
:   NO2 + SO2 → NO + SO3 (fast)

The NO catalyst is regenerated. The overall rate is the rate of the slow step[[15]](#cite_note-Steinfeld-15)

:   v=2k1[NO]2[O2].

An example of [heterogeneous catalysis](/wiki/Heterogeneous_catalysis "Heterogeneous catalysis") is the reaction of [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") and [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") on the surface of [titanium dioxide](/wiki/Titanium_dioxide "Titanium dioxide") (TiO2, or *titania*) to produce water. [Scanning tunneling microscopy](/wiki/Scanning_tunneling_microscopy "Scanning tunneling microscopy") showed that the molecules undergo [adsorption](/wiki/Adsorption "Adsorption") and [dissociation](/wiki/Dissociation_(chemistry) "Dissociation (chemistry)"). The dissociated, surface-bound O and H atoms [diffuse](/wiki/Diffusion "Diffusion") together. The intermediate reaction states are: HO2, H2O2, then H3O2 and the reaction product ([water molecule dimers](/wiki/Water_dimer "Water dimer")), after which the water molecule [desorbs](/wiki/Desorption "Desorption") from the catalyst surface.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Reaction energetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reaction energetics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/CatalysisScheme-en.svg/330px-CatalysisScheme-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:CatalysisScheme-en.svg)

Generic potential energy diagram showing the effect of a catalyst in a hypothetical exothermic chemical reaction X + Y to give Z. The presence of the catalyst opens a different reaction pathway (shown in red) with lower activation energy. The final result and the overall thermodynamics are the same.

Catalysts enable pathways that differ from those of uncatalyzed reactions. These pathways have lower [activation energy](/wiki/Activation_energy "Activation energy"). Consequently, more molecular collisions have the energy needed to reach the [transition state](/wiki/Transition_state "Transition state"). Hence, catalysts can enable reactions that would otherwise be blocked or slowed by a kinetic barrier. The catalyst may increase the reaction rate or selectivity, or enable the reaction at lower temperatures. This effect can be illustrated with an [energy profile](/wiki/Energy_profile_(chemistry) "Energy profile (chemistry)") diagram.

In the catalyzed [elementary reaction](/wiki/Elementary_reaction "Elementary reaction"), catalysts do **not** change the extent of a reaction: they have **no** effect on the [chemical equilibrium](/wiki/Chemical_equilibrium "Chemical equilibrium") of a reaction. The ratio of the forward and the reverse reaction rates is unaffected (see also [thermodynamics](/wiki/Thermodynamics "Thermodynamics")). The [second law of thermodynamics](/wiki/Second_law_of_thermodynamics "Second law of thermodynamics") describes why a catalyst does not change the chemical equilibrium of a reaction. Suppose there was such a catalyst that shifted an equilibrium. Introducing the catalyst to the system would result in a reaction to move to the new equilibrium, producing energy. Production of energy is a necessary result since reactions are spontaneous only if [Gibbs free energy](/wiki/Gibbs_free_energy "Gibbs free energy") is produced, and if there is no energy barrier, there is no need for a catalyst. Then, removing the catalyst would also result in a reaction, producing energy; i.e. the addition and its reverse process, removal, would both produce energy. Thus, a catalyst that could change the equilibrium would be a [perpetual motion machine](/wiki/Perpetual_motion_machine "Perpetual motion machine"), a contradiction to the laws of thermodynamics.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Thus, catalysts **do not** alter the equilibrium constant. (A catalyst can however change the equilibrium concentrations by reacting in a subsequent step. It is then consumed as the reaction proceeds, and thus it is also a reactant. Illustrative is the base-catalyzed [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") of [esters](/wiki/Ester "Ester"), where the produced [carboxylic acid](/wiki/Carboxylic_acid "Carboxylic acid") immediately reacts with the base catalyst and thus the reaction equilibrium is shifted towards hydrolysis.)

The catalyst stabilizes the transition state more than it stabilizes the starting material. It decreases the kinetic barrier by decreasing the *difference* in energy between starting material and the transition state. It **does not** change the energy difference between starting materials and products (thermodynamic barrier), or the available energy (this is provided by the environment as heat or light).

### Related concepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Related concepts")]

Some so-called catalysts are really **[precatalysts](/wiki/Precatalyst "Precatalyst")**, which convert to catalysts in the reaction. For example, [Wilkinson's catalyst](/wiki/Wilkinson%27s_catalyst "Wilkinson's catalyst") RhCl(PPh3)3 loses one triphenylphosphine ligand before entering the true catalytic cycle. Precatalysts are easier to store but are easily activated [in situ](/wiki/In_situ#Chemistry_and_chemical_engineering "In situ"). Because of this preactivation step, many catalytic reactions involve an [induction period](/wiki/Induction_period "Induction period").

In **cooperative catalysis**, chemical species that improve catalytic activity are called **cocatalysts** or **promoters**.

In [tandem catalysis](/wiki/Tandem_catalysis "Tandem catalysis") two or more different catalysts are coupled in a one-pot reaction.

In [autocatalysis](/wiki/Autocatalysis "Autocatalysis"), the catalyst *is* a product of the overall reaction, in contrast to all other types of catalysis considered in this article. The simplest example of autocatalysis is a reaction of type A + B → 2 B, in one or in several steps. The overall reaction is just A → B, so that B is a product. But since B is also a reactant, it may be present in the rate equation and affect the reaction rate. As the reaction proceeds, the concentration of B increases and can accelerate the reaction as a catalyst. In effect, the reaction accelerates itself or is autocatalyzed. An example is the [hydrolysis](/wiki/Hydrolysis "Hydrolysis") of an [ester](/wiki/Ester "Ester") such as [aspirin](/wiki/Aspirin "Aspirin") to a [carboxylic acid](/wiki/Carboxylic_acid "Carboxylic acid") and an [alcohol](/wiki/Alcohol_(chemistry) "Alcohol (chemistry)"). In the absence of added acid catalysts, the carboxylic acid product catalyzes the hydrolysis.

**Switchable catalysis** refers to a type of catalysis where the catalyst can be toggled between different ground states possessing distinct reactivity, typically by applying an external stimulus.[[19]](#cite_note-19) This ability to reversibly switch the catalyst allows for spatiotemporal control over catalytic activity and selectivity. The external stimuli used to switch the catalyst can include changes in temperature, pH, light,[[20]](#cite_note-20) electric fields, or the addition of chemical agents.

A true catalyst can work in tandem with a [sacrificial catalyst](/wiki/Catalytic_cycle#Sacrificial_catalysts "Catalytic cycle"). The true catalyst is consumed in the elementary reaction and turned into a deactivated form.
The sacrificial catalyst regenerates the true catalyst for another cycle. The sacrificial catalyst is consumed in the reaction, and as such, it is not really a catalyst, but a reagent. For example, [osmium tetroxide](/wiki/Osmium_tetroxide "Osmium tetroxide") (OsO4) is a good reagent for dihydroxylation, but it is highly toxic and expensive. In [Upjohn dihydroxylation](/wiki/Upjohn_dihydroxylation "Upjohn dihydroxylation"), the sacrificial catalyst [N-methylmorpholine N-oxide](/wiki/N-methylmorpholine_N-oxide "N-methylmorpholine N-oxide") (NMMO) regenerates OsO4, and only catalytic quantities of OsO4 are needed.

### Classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Classification")]

Catalysis may be classified as either [homogeneous or heterogeneous](/wiki/Homogeneity_and_heterogeneity "Homogeneity and heterogeneity"). A [homogeneous catalysis](/wiki/Homogeneous_catalysis "Homogeneous catalysis") is one whose components are dispersed in the same phase (usually gaseous or liquid) as the [reactant](/wiki/Reactant "Reactant")'s molecules. A [heterogeneous catalysis](/wiki/Heterogeneous_catalysis "Heterogeneous catalysis") is one where the reaction components are not in the same phase. [Enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") and other biocatalysts are often considered as a third category. Similar mechanistic principles apply to heterogeneous, homogeneous, and biocatalysis.

## Heterogeneous catalysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Heterogeneous catalysis")]

Main article: [Heterogeneous catalysis](/wiki/Heterogeneous_catalysis "Heterogeneous catalysis")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Zeolite-ZSM-5-vdW.png/250px-Zeolite-ZSM-5-vdW.png)](/wiki/File:Zeolite-ZSM-5-vdW.png)

The microporous molecular structure of the [zeolite](/wiki/Zeolite "Zeolite") ZSM-5 is exploited in catalysts used in refineries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Ceolite_nax.JPG/250px-Ceolite_nax.JPG)](/wiki/File:Ceolite_nax.JPG)

Zeolites are extruded as pellets for easy handling in catalytic reactors.

Heterogeneous catalysts act in a different [phase](/wiki/Phase_(matter) "Phase (matter)") than the [reactants](/wiki/Reactants "Reactants"). Most heterogeneous catalysts are [solids](/wiki/Solid "Solid") that act on substrates in a [liquid](/wiki/Liquid "Liquid") or gaseous [reaction mixture](/wiki/Reaction_mixture "Reaction mixture"). Important heterogeneous catalysts include [zeolites](/wiki/Zeolite "Zeolite"), [alumina](/wiki/Alumina "Alumina"),[[21]](#cite_note-21) higher-order oxides, graphitic carbon, [transition metal](/wiki/Transition_metal "Transition metal") [oxides](/wiki/Oxide "Oxide"), metals such as [Raney nickel](/wiki/Raney_nickel "Raney nickel") for hydrogenation, and [vanadium(V) oxide](/wiki/Vanadium(V)_oxide "Vanadium(V) oxide") for oxidation of [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") into [sulfur trioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_trioxide "Sulfur trioxide") by the [contact process](/wiki/Contact_process "Contact process").[[22]](#cite_note-Housecroft-22)

Diverse mechanisms for [reactions on surfaces](/wiki/Reactions_on_surfaces "Reactions on surfaces") are known, depending on how the adsorption takes place ([Langmuir-Hinshelwood](/wiki/Langmuir-Hinshelwood-Hougen-Watson "Langmuir-Hinshelwood-Hougen-Watson"), [Eley-Rideal](/wiki/Eley%E2%80%93Rideal_mechanism "Eley–Rideal mechanism"), and Mars-[van Krevelen](/wiki/Dirk_Willem_van_Krevelen "Dirk Willem van Krevelen")).[[23]](#cite_note-doi-23) The total surface area of a solid has an important effect on the reaction rate. The smaller the catalyst particle size, the larger the surface area for a given mass of particles.

A heterogeneous catalyst has **active sites**, which are the atoms or crystal faces where the substrate actually binds. Active sites are atoms but are often described as a facet (edge, surface, step, etc.) of a solid. Most of the volume but also most of the surface of a heterogeneous catalyst may be catalytically inactive. Finding out the nature of the active site is technically challenging.

For example, the catalyst for the [Haber process](/wiki/Haber_process "Haber process") for the synthesis of [ammonia](/wiki/Ammonia "Ammonia") from [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") and [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") is often described as [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"). But detailed studies and many optimizations have led to catalysts that are mixtures of iron-potassium-calcium-aluminum-oxide.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The reacting [gases](/wiki/Gas "Gas") [adsorb](/wiki/Adsorption "Adsorption") onto active sites on the iron particles. Once physically adsorbed, the reagents partially or wholly dissociate and form new bonds. In this way the particularly strong [triple bond](/wiki/Triple_bond "Triple bond") in nitrogen is broken, which would be extremely uncommon in the gas phase due to its high activation energy. Thus, the activation energy of the overall reaction is lowered, and the rate of reaction increases.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Another place where a heterogeneous catalyst is applied is in the oxidation of sulfur dioxide on [vanadium(V) oxide](/wiki/Vanadium(V)_oxide "Vanadium(V) oxide") for the production of [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid").[[22]](#cite_note-Housecroft-22) Many heterogeneous catalysts are in fact nanomaterials.

Heterogeneous catalysts are typically "[supported](/wiki/Catalyst_support "Catalyst support")", which means that the catalyst is dispersed on a second material that enhances the effectiveness or minimizes its cost. Supports prevent or minimize agglomeration and sintering of small catalyst particles, exposing more surface area, thus catalysts have a higher specific activity (per gram) on support. Sometimes the support is merely a surface on which the catalyst is spread to increase the surface area. More often, the support and the catalyst interact, affecting the catalytic reaction. Supports can also be used in nanoparticle synthesis by providing sites for individual molecules of catalyst to chemically bind. Supports are porous materials with a high surface area, most commonly [alumina](/wiki/Alumina "Alumina"), [zeolites](/wiki/Zeolites "Zeolites"), or various kinds of [activated carbon](/wiki/Activated_carbon "Activated carbon"). Specialized supports include [silicon dioxide](/wiki/Silicon_dioxide "Silicon dioxide"), [titanium dioxide](/wiki/Titanium_dioxide "Titanium dioxide"), [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate"), and [barium sulfate](/wiki/Barium_sulfate "Barium sulfate").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Electrocatalysts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Electrocatalysts")]

Main article: [Electrocatalyst](/wiki/Electrocatalyst "Electrocatalyst")

In the context of [electrochemistry](/wiki/Electrochemistry "Electrochemistry"), specifically in [fuel cell](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") engineering, various metal-containing catalysts are used to enhance the rates of the [half reactions](/wiki/Half_reaction "Half reaction") that comprise the fuel cell. One common type of fuel cell electrocatalyst is based upon [nanoparticles](/wiki/Nanoparticles "Nanoparticles") of [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") that are supported on slightly larger [carbon](/wiki/Carbon "Carbon") particles. When in contact with one of the [electrodes](/wiki/Electrode "Electrode") in a fuel cell, this platinum increases the rate of [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen") reduction either to water or to [hydroxide](/wiki/Hydroxide "Hydroxide") or [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide").

## Homogeneous catalysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Homogeneous catalysis")]

Main article: [Homogeneous catalysis](/wiki/Homogeneous_catalysis "Homogeneous catalysis")

Homogeneous catalysts function in the same phase as the reactants. Typically homogeneous catalysts are dissolved in a solvent with the substrates. One example of homogeneous catalysis involves the influence of [H](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen")+ on the [esterification](/wiki/Esterification "Esterification") of carboxylic acids, such as the formation of [methyl acetate](/wiki/Methyl_acetate "Methyl acetate") from [acetic acid](/wiki/Acetic_acid "Acetic acid") and [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol").[[27]](#cite_note-27) High-volume processes requiring a homogeneous catalyst include [hydroformylation](/wiki/Hydroformylation "Hydroformylation"), [hydrosilylation](/wiki/Hydrosilylation "Hydrosilylation"), [hydrocyanation](/wiki/Hydrocyanation "Hydrocyanation"). For inorganic chemists, homogeneous catalysis is often synonymous with [organometallic catalysts](/wiki/Organometallic_chemistry "Organometallic chemistry").[[28]](#cite_note-28) Many homogeneous catalysts are however not organometallic, illustrated by the use of cobalt salts that catalyze the oxidation of [p-xylene](/wiki/P-xylene "P-xylene") to [terephthalic acid](/wiki/Terephthalic_acid "Terephthalic acid").

### Organocatalysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Organocatalysis")]

Main article: [Organocatalysis](/wiki/Organocatalysis "Organocatalysis")

Whereas transition metals sometimes attract most of the attention in the study of catalysis, small organic molecules without metals can also exhibit catalytic properties, as is apparent from the fact that many [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") lack transition metals. Typically, organic catalysts require a higher loading (amount of catalyst per unit amount of reactant, expressed in [mol%](/wiki/Mol%25 "Mol%") [amount of substance](/wiki/Amount_of_substance "Amount of substance")) than transition metal(-ion)-based catalysts, but these catalysts are usually commercially available in bulk, helping to lower costs. In the early 2000s, these organocatalysts were considered "new generation" and are competitive to traditional [metal](/wiki/Metal "Metal")(-ion)-containing catalysts.

Organocatalysts are supposed to operate akin to metal-free enzymes utilizing, e.g., noncovalent interactions such as [hydrogen bonding](/wiki/Hydrogen_bonding "Hydrogen bonding"). The discipline organocatalysis is divided into the application of covalent (e.g., [proline](/wiki/Proline "Proline"), [DMAP](/wiki/4-Dimethylaminopyridine "4-Dimethylaminopyridine")) and noncovalent (e.g., [thiourea organocatalysis](/wiki/Thiourea_organocatalysis "Thiourea organocatalysis")) organocatalysts referring to the preferred catalyst-[substrate](/wiki/Substrate_(chemistry) "Substrate (chemistry)") [binding](/wiki/Binding_(molecular) "Binding (molecular)") and interaction, respectively. The Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2021 was awarded jointly to Benjamin List and David W.C. MacMillan "for the development of asymmetric organocatalysis."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Photocatalysts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Photocatalysts")]

Main article: [Photocatalysis](/wiki/Photocatalysis "Photocatalysis")

Photocatalysis is the phenomenon where the catalyst can receive light to generate an [excited state](/wiki/Excited_state "Excited state") that effect redox reactions.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Singlet oxygen](/wiki/Singlet_oxygen "Singlet oxygen") is usually produced by photocatalysis. Photocatalysts are components of [dye-sensitized solar cells](/wiki/Dye-sensitized_solar_cell "Dye-sensitized solar cell").

### Enzymes and biocatalysts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Enzymes and biocatalysts")]

Main article: [Enzyme catalysis](/wiki/Enzyme_catalysis "Enzyme catalysis")

In biology, [enzymes](/wiki/Enzyme "Enzyme") are protein-based catalysts in [metabolism](/wiki/Metabolism "Metabolism") and [catabolism](/wiki/Catabolism "Catabolism"). Most biocatalysts are enzymes, but other nonprotein-based classes of biomolecules also exhibit catalytic properties including [ribozymes](/wiki/Ribozyme "Ribozyme"), and synthetic [deoxyribozymes](/wiki/Deoxyribozyme "Deoxyribozyme").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Biocatalysts can be thought of as an intermediate between homogeneous and heterogeneous catalysts, although strictly speaking soluble enzymes are homogeneous catalysts and [membrane](/wiki/Biological_membrane "Biological membrane")-bound enzymes are heterogeneous. Several factors affect the activity of enzymes (and other catalysts) including temperature, pH, the concentration of enzymes, substrate, and products. A particularly important reagent in enzymatic reactions is water, which is the product of many bond-forming reactions and a reactant in many bond-breaking processes.

In [biocatalysis](/wiki/Biocatalysis "Biocatalysis"), enzymes are employed to prepare many commodity chemicals including [high-fructose corn syrup](/wiki/High-fructose_corn_syrup "High-fructose corn syrup") and [acrylamide](/wiki/Acrylamide "Acrylamide").

Some [monoclonal antibodies](/wiki/Monoclonal_antibodies "Monoclonal antibodies") whose binding target is a stable molecule that resembles the transition state of a chemical reaction can function as weak catalysts for that chemical reaction by lowering its activation energy.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Such catalytic antibodies are sometimes called "[abzymes](/wiki/Abzyme "Abzyme")".

## Significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Significance")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Verbrennung_eines_Zuckerw%C3%BCrfels.png/250px-Verbrennung_eines_Zuckerw%C3%BCrfels.png)](/wiki/File:Verbrennung_eines_Zuckerw%C3%BCrfels.png)

Left: Partially caramelized [cube sugar](/wiki/Cube_sugar "Cube sugar"), Right: burning cube sugar with ash as catalyst



A Ti-Cr-Pt tube (~40 μm long) releases oxygen bubbles when immersed in [hydrogen peroxide](/wiki/Hydrogen_peroxide "Hydrogen peroxide") (via catalytic decomposition), forming a [micropump](/wiki/Micropump "Micropump").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Estimates are that 90% of all commercially produced chemical products involve catalysts at some stage in the process of their manufacture. In 2005, catalytic processes generated about $900 billion in products worldwide.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Catalysis is so pervasive that subareas are not readily classified. Some areas of particular concentration are surveyed below.

### Energy processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Energy processing")]

[Petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") refining makes intensive use of catalysis for [alkylation](/wiki/Alkylation "Alkylation"), [catalytic cracking](/wiki/Catalytic_cracking "Catalytic cracking") (breaking long-chain hydrocarbons into smaller pieces), [naphtha](/wiki/Petroleum_naphtha "Petroleum naphtha") reforming and [steam reforming](/wiki/Steam_reforming "Steam reforming") (conversion of [hydrocarbons](/wiki/Hydrocarbon "Hydrocarbon") into [synthesis gas](/wiki/Synthesis_gas "Synthesis gas")). Even the exhaust from the burning of fossil fuels is treated via catalysis: [Catalytic converters](/wiki/Catalytic_converter "Catalytic converter"), typically composed of [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") and [rhodium](/wiki/Rhodium "Rhodium"), break down some of the more harmful byproducts of automobile exhaust.

:   2 CO + 2 NO → 2 CO2 + N2

With regard to synthetic fuels, an old but still important process is the [Fischer–Tropsch synthesis](/wiki/Fischer%E2%80%93Tropsch_synthesis "Fischer–Tropsch synthesis") of hydrocarbons from [synthesis gas](/wiki/Synthesis_gas "Synthesis gas"), which itself is processed via [water-gas shift reactions](/wiki/Water_gas_shift_reaction "Water gas shift reaction"), catalyzed by iron. The [Sabatier reaction](/wiki/Sabatier_reaction "Sabatier reaction") produces [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") from carbon dioxide and hydrogen. [Biodiesel](/wiki/Biodiesel "Biodiesel") and related biofuels require processing via both inorganic and biocatalysts.

[Fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") rely on catalysts for both the anodic and cathodic reactions.

[Catalytic heaters](/wiki/Catalytic_heater "Catalytic heater") generate flameless heat from a supply of combustible fuel.

### Bulk chemicals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bulk chemicals")]

[![Typical vanadium pentoxide catalyst used in sulfuric acid production for an intermediate reaction to convert sulfur dioxide to sulfur trioxide.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/CataylstExampleSulfuricAcidPlant.jpg/250px-CataylstExampleSulfuricAcidPlant.jpg)](/wiki/File:CataylstExampleSulfuricAcidPlant.jpg)

Typical vanadium pentoxide catalyst used in sulfuric acid production for an intermediate reaction to convert sulfur dioxide to sulfur trioxide.

Some of the largest-scale chemicals are produced via catalytic oxidation, often using [oxygen](/wiki/Oxygen "Oxygen"). Examples include [nitric acid](/wiki/Nitric_acid "Nitric acid") (from ammonia), [sulfuric acid](/wiki/Sulfuric_acid "Sulfuric acid") (from [sulfur dioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_dioxide "Sulfur dioxide") to [sulfur trioxide](/wiki/Sulfur_trioxide "Sulfur trioxide") by the [contact process](/wiki/Contact_process "Contact process")), [terephthalic acid](/wiki/Terephthalic_acid "Terephthalic acid") from p-xylene, [acrylic acid](/wiki/Acrylic_acid "Acrylic acid") from [propylene](/wiki/Propylene "Propylene") or [propane](/wiki/Propane "Propane") and [acrylonitrile](/wiki/Acrylonitrile "Acrylonitrile") from propane and ammonia.[[23]](#cite_note-doi-23)

The production of ammonia is one of the largest-scale and most energy-intensive processes. In the [Haber process](/wiki/Haber_process "Haber process") [nitrogen](/wiki/Nitrogen "Nitrogen") is combined with hydrogen over an iron oxide catalyst.[[35]](#cite_note-Smil_2004_Enriching-35) [Methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol") is prepared from [carbon monoxide](/wiki/Carbon_monoxide "Carbon monoxide") or carbon dioxide but using copper-zinc catalysts.

Bulk polymers derived from [ethylene](/wiki/Ethylene "Ethylene") and [propylene](/wiki/Propylene "Propylene") are often prepared using [Ziegler–Natta catalyst](/wiki/Ziegler%E2%80%93Natta_catalyst "Ziegler–Natta catalyst"). Polyesters, polyamides, and [isocyanates](/wiki/Isocyanate "Isocyanate") are derived via [acid–base catalysis](/wiki/Acid%E2%80%93base_catalysis "Acid–base catalysis").

Most [carbonylation](/wiki/Carbonylation "Carbonylation") processes require metal catalysts, examples include the [Monsanto acetic acid process](/wiki/Monsanto_acetic_acid_process "Monsanto acetic acid process") and [hydroformylation](/wiki/Hydroformylation "Hydroformylation").

### Fine chemicals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Fine chemicals")]

Many [fine chemicals](/wiki/Fine_chemicals "Fine chemicals") are prepared via catalysis; methods include those of heavy industry as well as more specialized processes that would be prohibitively expensive on a large scale. Examples include the [Heck reaction](/wiki/Heck_reaction "Heck reaction"), and [Friedel–Crafts reactions](/wiki/Friedel%E2%80%93Crafts_reaction "Friedel–Crafts reaction"). Because most bioactive compounds are [chiral](/wiki/Chirality_(chemistry) "Chirality (chemistry)"), many pharmaceuticals are produced by enantioselective catalysis (catalytic [asymmetric synthesis](/wiki/Asymmetric_synthesis "Asymmetric synthesis")). (R)-1,2-Propandiol, the precursor to the antibacterial [levofloxacin](/wiki/Levofloxacin "Levofloxacin"), can be synthesized efficiently from hydroxyacetone by using catalysts based on [BINAP](/wiki/BINAP "BINAP")-ruthenium complexes, in [Noyori asymmetric hydrogenation](/wiki/Noyori_asymmetric_hydrogenation "Noyori asymmetric hydrogenation"):[[36]](#cite_note-Gordon-36)

[![levofloxaxin synthesis](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/ca/Levofloxacin3.png)](/wiki/File:Levofloxacin3.png "levofloxaxin synthesis")

levofloxaxin synthesis

### Food processing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Food processing")]

One of the most obvious applications of catalysis is the [hydrogenation](/wiki/Hydrogenation "Hydrogenation") (reaction with [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") gas) of fats using [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel") catalyst to produce [margarine](/wiki/Margarine "Margarine").[[37]](#cite_note-37) Many other foodstuffs are prepared via biocatalysis (see below).

### Environment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Environment")]

Catalysis affects the environment by increasing the efficiency of industrial processes, but catalysis also plays a direct role in the environment. A notable example is the catalytic role of [chlorine](/wiki/Chlorine "Chlorine") [free radicals](/wiki/Free_radical "Free radical") in the breakdown of [ozone](/wiki/Ozone "Ozone"). These radicals are formed by the action of [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") [radiation](/wiki/Radiation "Radiation") on [chlorofluorocarbons](/wiki/Chlorofluorocarbon "Chlorofluorocarbon") (CFCs).

:   Cl**·** + O3 → ClO**·** + O2
:   ClO**·** + O· → Cl**·** + O2

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: History")]

The term "catalyst", broadly defined as anything that increases the rate of a process, is derived from [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language") [καταλύειν](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%CE%BA%CE%B1%CF%84%CE%B1%CE%BB%CF%8D%CF%89 "wikt:καταλύω"), meaning "to annul", or "to untie", or "to pick up". The concept of catalysis was invented by chemist [Elizabeth Fulhame](/wiki/Elizabeth_Fulhame "Elizabeth Fulhame") and described in a 1794 book, based on her novel work in oxidation–reduction reactions.[[8]](#cite_note-Laidler-8)[[9]](#cite_note-American_Chemical_Society-9)[[38]](#cite_note-38) The first chemical reaction in organic chemistry that knowingly used a catalyst was studied in 1811 by [Gottlieb Kirchhoff](/wiki/Gottlieb_Kirchhoff "Gottlieb Kirchhoff"), who discovered the acid-catalyzed conversion of starch to glucose. The term *catalysis* was later used by [Jöns Jakob Berzelius](/wiki/J%C3%B6ns_Jakob_Berzelius "Jöns Jakob Berzelius") in 1835[[39]](#cite_note-39) to describe reactions that are accelerated by substances that remain unchanged after the reaction. [Fulhame](/wiki/Elizabeth_Fulhame "Elizabeth Fulhame"), who predated Berzelius, did work with water as opposed to metals in her reduction experiments. Other 18th century chemists who worked in catalysis were [Eilhard Mitscherlich](/wiki/Eilhard_Mitscherlich "Eilhard Mitscherlich")[[40]](#cite_note-40) who referred to it as *contact* processes, and [Johann Wolfgang Döbereiner](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_D%C3%B6bereiner "Johann Wolfgang Döbereiner")[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) who spoke of *contact action.* He developed [Döbereiner's lamp](/wiki/D%C3%B6bereiner%27s_lamp "Döbereiner's lamp"), a [lighter](/wiki/Lighter_(fire_starter) "Lighter (fire starter)") based on [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen") and a [platinum](/wiki/Platinum "Platinum") sponge, which became a commercial success in the 1820s that lives on today. [Humphry Davy](/wiki/Humphry_Davy "Humphry Davy") discovered the use of platinum in catalysis.[[43]](#cite_note-43) In the 1880s, [Wilhelm Ostwald](/wiki/Wilhelm_Ostwald "Wilhelm Ostwald") at [Leipzig University](/wiki/Leipzig_University "Leipzig University") started a systematic investigation into reactions that were catalyzed by the presence of acids and bases, and found that chemical reactions occur at finite rates and that these rates can be used to determine the strengths of acids and bases. For this work, Ostwald was awarded the 1909 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry").[[44]](#cite_note-44) [Vladimir Ipatieff](/wiki/Vladimir_Ipatieff "Vladimir Ipatieff") performed some of the earliest industrial scale reactions, including the discovery and commercialization of oligomerization and the development of catalysts for hydrogenation.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Inhibitors, poisons, and promoters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Inhibitors, poisons, and promoters")]

An added substance that lowers the rate is called a [reaction inhibitor](/wiki/Reaction_inhibitor "Reaction inhibitor") if reversible and [catalyst poisons](/wiki/Catalyst_poisoning "Catalyst poisoning") if irreversible.[[1]](#cite_note-IUPACc-1) Promoters are substances that increase the catalytic activity, even though they are not catalysts by themselves.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Inhibitors are sometimes referred to as "negative catalysts" since they decrease the reaction rate.[[47]](#cite_note-Laidler78-47) However the term inhibitor is preferred since they do not work by introducing a reaction path with higher activation energy; this would not lower the rate since the reaction would continue to occur by the noncatalyzed path. Instead, they act either by deactivating catalysts or by removing reaction intermediates such as free radicals.[[47]](#cite_note-Laidler78-47)[[12]](#cite_note-LM82-12) In [heterogeneous](/wiki/Heterogeneous "Heterogeneous") catalysis, [coking](/wiki/Coking "Coking") inhibits the catalyst, which becomes covered by [polymeric](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") side products.

The inhibitor may modify selectivity in addition to rate. For instance, in the hydrogenation of [alkynes](/wiki/Alkyne "Alkyne") to [alkenes](/wiki/Alkene "Alkene"), a [palladium](/wiki/Palladium "Palladium") (Pd) catalyst partly "poisoned" with [lead(II) acetate](/wiki/Lead(II)_acetate "Lead(II) acetate") (Pb(CH3CO2)2) can be used ([Lindlar catalyst](/wiki/Lindlar_catalyst "Lindlar catalyst")).[[48]](#cite_note-Lindlar-48) Without the deactivation of the catalyst, the alkene produced would be further hydrogenated to [alkane](/wiki/Alkane "Alkane").[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

The inhibitor can produce this effect by, e.g., selectively poisoning only certain types of active sites. Another mechanism is the modification of surface geometry. For instance, in hydrogenation operations, large planes of metal surface function as sites of [hydrogenolysis](/wiki/Hydrogenolysis "Hydrogenolysis") catalysis while sites catalyzing [hydrogenation](/wiki/Hydrogenation "Hydrogenation") of unsaturates are smaller. Thus, a poison that covers the surface randomly will tend to lower the number of uncontaminated large planes but leave proportionally smaller sites free, thus changing the hydrogenation vs. hydrogenolysis selectivity. Many other mechanisms are also possible.

Promoters can cover up the surface to prevent the production of a mat of coke, or even actively remove such material (e.g., rhenium on platinum in [platforming](/wiki/Catalytic_reforming "Catalytic reforming")). They can aid the dispersion of the catalytic material or bind to reagents.

## Prebiotic catalysis in the origin of life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Catalysis&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Prebiotic catalysis in the origin of life")]

[Life](/wiki/Life "Life") is based on an interplay between information processing and catalytic activity carried out by biological polymers.[[51]](#cite_note-Tkachenko2024-51) A possible [evolutionary](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") pathway for the emergence of catalytic functions in prebiotic information coding [polymers](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer") was proposed.[[51]](#cite_note-Tkachenko2024-51) It has also been proposed that life emerged as an [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA")-[protein](/wiki/Protein "Protein") system in which the two components cross catalyzed the formation of each other.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
